content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Selection reform is now live government policy with EU tailwind but faces low-probability cross-party durability, high reversal risk on government turnover, and non-negotiable stakeholder preconditions (workforce guarantees, statutory funding) that the minister has not yet addressed, making it politically feasible in the medium term (2027–2028) only if the government narrows its scope to pilot-phase rebalancing coupled with explicit quality-levelling commitments, abandons abolition rhetoric, and negotiates binding funding guarantees—the opposite of which suggests either stalled-indefinite consultation or legislative reversal within one electoral cycle.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: The Tisza Party's 12 April 2026 electoral victory (141 of 199 seats, ~53% party-list) created a constitutional two-thirds mandate; education received a separate ministry for the first time since 2010, signalling structural reform is now institutionally feasible.

Education Minister Lannert Judit (June 2026) publicly stated that 6/8-year gimnázium students do not develop faster than age-cohort peers; the system's only function is elite self-selection and socioeconomic status reproduction, not pedagogy. She framed the question as a societal-policy choice, not technical, and explicitly committed to review rather than abolition, with current gimnáziums to retain their position.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: Will the government publish a formal legislative timetable, Oktatási Hivatal review mandate, or government resolution on selection reform, moving it beyond consultative intent?

Are statutory (vs annual-budget) funding-floor guarantees politically achievable under the projected fiscal consolidation pressures, or will compensatory funding remain contingent on annual budget cycles?
